title: Single-Point-of-Failure Inventory

content.statement: What cannot fail safely is a hidden existential risk.
content.question: Name your business's top 3 single points of failure. What's your mitigation for each?
content.equation: SPF = Σ(Criticality × Concentration × Recovery Difficulty × Time-to-Recover)
sop: List every person, platform, supplier, or customer that, if it disappeared tomorrow, would stop something critical.
Rank them by how hard and how long recovery would take.
For your top single point of failure, build one real backup this month, not just a plan to build one someday.
Rate Criticality, Concentration, Recovery Difficulty, and Time-to-Recover 1-10 for your top risk (see calculation, page 6)
Re-run the inventory every 6 months, new single points of failure appear as you grow, a new key hire, a new dominant platform.
content.explainer: SPoFs are the risks you pretend aren't there. Listing them is 80% of the work.
questions: Name your business's top 3 single points of failure. What's your mitigation for each?
If your top customer, supplier, or platform vanished tomorrow, how many days before it actually hurt?
Which single point of failure have you known about for months but haven't actually fixed?

definitions.def: One thing whose breakage would stop everything.

content.equation_example: Rate Criticality, Concentration, Recovery Difficulty, and Time-to-Recover 1-10 each for your top risk. Criticality 9, Concentration 8, Recovery Difficulty 7, Time-to-Recover 6 → SPF = 9×8×7×6 = 3,024 out of a possible 10,000. Higher SPF means that single point of failure could genuinely end the business; lower means you'd feel it but survive it.
content.closing_statement: A backup you haven't built yet isn't a backup.

caption:
Principle Nº 43: Single-Point-of-Failure Inventory

What cannot fail safely is a hidden existential risk.

Ask yourself: Name your business's top 3 single points of failure. What's your mitigation for each?
